title: Rapidly Growing Mass on Cat's Shoulder: Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat's shoulder mass growing fast? Learn about potential causes, why this is a high-priority vet visit, and how photos help triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: A rapidly growing mass on a cat's shoulder is considered high urgency and requires a veterinary examination within 24 to 48 hours to rule out aggressive tumors or infection. Early detection is critical for improving the success of surgical treatment and long-term recovery.

What could a rapidly growing soft mass on my cat's shoulder be?

A rapidly growing soft mass on a cat's shoulder can represent several different medical conditions. Common possibilities include an abscess (a pocket of infection), a fluid-filled cyst, or a tumor. In cats, veterinarians are particularly observant of masses in the shoulder or hip area due to the risk of Injection Site Sarcomas (ISS), which are aggressive tumors that can develop where a vaccine or medication was previously administered. Only a veterinarian can determine the exact nature of the growth through diagnostic testing.

Is a rapidly growing shoulder mass in a cat an emergency?

  • The urgency level for this symptom is High; while the cat may seem otherwise healthy, a rapidly growing mass is a significant clinical red flag.
  • You should contact your veterinarian to schedule an exam within the next 24 to 48 hours.
  • Early detection is vital, especially for aggressive growths, as it significantly increases the success rate of surgical removal and long-term recovery.

How does taking a photo of the mass help the veterinary triage process?

  • A clear, well-lit photo allows the triage staff to visualize the exact location, size, and appearance (such as redness or discharge) before you arrive.
  • Because these masses can change size daily, a photo serves as a baseline to help the doctor track the rate of growth.
  • It provides a visual record for the doctor to compare the size of the mass from when you first noticed it to the time of the physical exam.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Upon detection of a rapidly growing mass on a cat's shoulder, initial triage should include assessment for mammary tumors, especially in neutered cats or those given progestational drugs, but also consider other differentials. Palpate regional lymph nodes to assess for spread. Obtain thoracic radiographs (ventrodorsal and lateral views) to detect pulmonary metastasis. Fine-needle aspirates may help differentiate inflammatory from neoplastic lesions, but histopathology is essential for definitive diagnosis, treatment planning, and prognosis. Consider bone tumors, especially if lameness or pathologic fracture is present; radiography will reveal osteolysis, proliferation, and soft-tissue swelling, and biopsy is imperative for confirmation. Mast cell tumors should be considered, particularly in cats older than 4 years, and surgical excision is the treatment of choice.

Chapter: Oncology, Surgery

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1404)

faqs.question: Should I try to drain or squeeze the mass at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to squeeze or pop a mass. If it is an abscess, you may push the infection deeper into the tissue; if it is a tumor, trauma to the area can cause significant pain, inflammation, or bleeding.

faqs.question: What diagnostic tests will the vet perform?
faqs.answer: The most common first step is a Fine Needle Aspirate (FNA). The vet will use a small needle to collect cells from the mass and examine them under a microscope to check for signs of infection, inflammation, or cancerous cells.

faqs.question: Can a soft mass be cancerous?
faqs.answer: Yes. While many people assume cancerous tumors are always hard, some aggressive feline sarcomas can feel soft or 'squishy' to the touch, which is why professional evaluation is necessary regardless of the texture.
